text: Welcome to another installment of hernia repair videos. This is a robotic repair of a suprapubic hernia. The patient was a fifty-year-old woman with a history of traumatic pelvic fracture with multiple operations and complications. She had at one point in time had a plating of her obturator, eventually developed osteomyelitis, necessity of open removal of hardware. She presented with an EHS M-IV-V hernia, which involved her intestine and bladder.

so cross-sectional review uh the axials here you see essentially an untouched upper abdomen down to the umbilicus and then below the umbo you'll notice uh m four or five hernia again involving that bladder and intestine i think for these hernias in particular sagittal views are great and give you a much better assessment of the abdominal wall. And you can see the multifocal nature of the hernia. The dimensions measured roughly seven by nine centimeters. But of course, for these hernias, these atypical hernias can be challenging to fix as fixation is a definite problem. So case setup We were going to do a three-way bladder catheter if we needed to instill any fluids. We catered antibiotics to prior infections. And then we were going to do a transabdominal approach, either with a preperitoneal or retromuscular repair and an upper dock.

The surgeon performs a preperitoneal dissection, transitioning to retromuscular dissection around the prior midline and groin incisions. The thin peritoneum is visible, and the dissection extends down to the hernia, separating the hernia sac from the pseudosac. The upper portion of the hernia is clear of bladder involvement, while careful dissection is performed near the lower portion where the bladder is encountered. Areolar dissection planes are utilized to facilitate smooth reduction of the hernia sac, with visible separation of the bladder down to the inferior margin of the hernia.

The surgeon completes the midline dissection and proceeds with lateral dissection to expose the bilateral myopectineal orifices. On the right side, the round ligament is transected near the peritoneum, and the obliterated umbilical artery is identified and transected. On the left side, the round ligament is similarly transected. A large defect is visible, and the team discusses closure options, opting for a vertical closure.

The surgeon uses permanent V-lock sutures to plicate the hernia sac, reducing dead space and facilitating closure of the fascial edges. The linea alba is plicated cephalad, fortifying the abdominal wall. The rectus muscles are approximated, ensuring smooth defect closure. Mesh placement follows, with three pieces of macroporous polypropylene mesh covering bilateral groins and the midline. The mesh is fixated at multiple points, including Cooper's ligament, and fiber sealant is applied to enhance the mesh-tissue interface.
The surgeon closes the flap after reducing a large hernia, addressing fenestrations on the right side. The flap closure is smooth with no tension visible.
